game_name: Romance of the Three Kingdoms II

danger_signals.visual_form: Province control map display showing opponent color spread
danger_signals.meaning: Threat level increases as rivals gain territory and resources

danger_signals.visual_form: Reputation system visual indicator tied to character loyalty stat
danger_signals.meaning: Low reputation creates risk of character defection or reduced command effectiveness
Opponent coordination attempts indicate active military threat

safety_signals.visual_form: Map display showing owned provinces in unified color block
safety_signals.meaning: Secure territorial base reduces vulnerability to multi-front attacks

safety_signals.visual_form: Character roster display with Intelligence, War Ability, Charm stats (10-100 range)
safety_signals.meaning: Strong officers with high stats provide reliable task execution and stability

safety_signals.visual_form: Adviser interface showing Intelligence stat at 100
safety_signals.meaning: Accurate prediction capability reduces strategic risk and planning failure

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Northern provinces, historical Cao Cao allegiance territories

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Custom characters placed by player or AI

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Initial map configuration shows Luoyang control with fragmented warlord territories
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI warlords exhibit power-consolidation behavior; central authority collapse

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple factions shown with roughly equal territorial distribution
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Aggressive expansion behavior from all AI lords; frequent military conflicts

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Scenario 6: Rise of Wei, Wu, Shu (AD 220)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Three major color-coded faction blocks dominating map with clear territorial boundaries
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Entrenched defensive postures; established supply lines and officer loyalty systems

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player faction color expands across majority of map provinces (41 total)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Victory condition triggered; remaining independent factions exhibit desperate resistance

environmental_storytelling.example: Province map with color-coding by warlord shows political fragmentation or consolidation state at glance
Six discrete starting scenarios reference actual Three Kingdoms novel events (Dong Zhuo's coup, empire division), establishing context without narrative cutscenes
200+ unique historical characters with individual Intelligence/War Ability/Charm stats (10-100) implicitly communicate their historical role and capability level
Land value mechanic indicates economic development and strategic importance; visual wealth representation through province status

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Remains loyal; accepts orders reliably; statistical bonuses to task success
Risk of defection; reduced task effectiveness; vulnerability to enemy recruitment
Adviser provides accurate success prediction; strategic decisions appear more calculated
Visual boost to character capabilities; improved duel success rates and task execution
Player can disrupt enemy coordination; blocks communication-dependent multi-province actions

notes: Romance of the Three Kingdoms II uses abstract turn-based strategy visualization rather than real-time action. World-state communication relies on: (1) province ownership map color display for territorial power balance, (2) character roster stat visibility for faction strength assessment, (3) reputation/loyalty system for internal stability signals, (4) scenario historical context for political situation framing. Limited source material restricts color/symbol/sigil specifics; however, the game's historical setting (Three Kingdoms period) and novel basis (Romance of the Three Kingdoms) provide implicit faction visual identity through territorial geography (Wei=North, Wu=South, Shu=West). Danger emerges from: territorial loss, officer disloyalty, multi-front attacks. Safety derives from: consolidation, high-stat officers, adviser accuracy.
sources_used: Wikipedia: Romance of the Three Kingdoms II
